question:
11. Sketch the curve
\[
y=\frac{x}{1+x^{6} \sin ^{2} x}
\]
and show that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x d x}{1+x^{6} \sin ^{2} x}
\]
exists.
solution:
Solution. Let
\[
f(x)=\frac{x}{1+x^{6} \sin ^{2} x}
\]

Then \( f \) is an odd function, so its graph is symmetric with respect to the origin; hence we need consider only non-negative values of \( x \).

Clearly
\[
x \geq \frac{x}{1+x^{6} \sin ^{2} x} \geq \frac{x}{1+x^{6}} .
\]

At points \( x=n \pi, n \) an integer, the curve is tangent to the line \( y=x \). At points \( x=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \), it is tangent to the graph of \( y=x /\left(1+x^{6}\right) \).

So the graph of \( f(x) \) oscillates between an upper curve \( y=x \) and a lower curve \( y=x /\left(1+x^{0}\right) \).

For large values of \( x \), except those very near an integral multiple of \( \pi \), \( f \) is very small, i.e., near the lower curve. Indeed, the graph is characterized by tall, narrow spikes at integral multiples of \( \pi \). (The spikes are much narrower than the drawing suggests. For example, the function \( f \) drops to less than .02 within .1 of \( 2 \pi \).)
Since \( f \) is non-negative on the whole domain of integration, the question of the convergence of
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} f(x) d x
\]
is equivalent to whether or not
\[
\int_{0}^{t} f(x) d x
\]
is bounded, and in turn to whether or not
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_{(n-1 / 2) \pi}^{(n+1 / 2) \pi} f(x) d x
\]
is convergent.
Note that \( |\sin t| \geq(2 / \pi)|t| \) for \( |t| \leq \pi / 2 \). Set \( k_{n}=2 \pi^{2}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3} \). Then, for any positive integer \( n \), and for
\[
\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \leq x \leq\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+x^{6} \sin ^{2} x & =1+x^{6} \sin ^{2}(x-n \pi) \\
& \geq 1+\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{6} \pi^{6} \frac{4}{\pi^{2}}(x-n \pi)^{2} \\
& =1+k_{n}^{2}(x-n \pi)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

So we get
\[
f(x) \leq \frac{\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi}{1+k_{n}^{2}(x-n \pi)^{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{r}
\int_{(n-1 / 2) \pi}^{(n+1 / 2) \pi} f(x) d x \leq\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\pi 2}^{\pi 2} \frac{d u}{1+k_{n}{ }^{2} u^{2}} \\
\leq\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d u}{1+k_{n}{ }^{2} u^{2}} \\
=\frac{\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi^{2}}{k_{n}} \leq \frac{A}{n^{2}}
\end{array}
\]
for some number \( A \) independent of \( n \).
Since \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1 / n^{2} \) is convergent, we conclude that
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_{(n-1 / 2) \pi}^{(n+1 / 2) \pi} f(x) d x
\]
is convergent.
Therefore \( \int_{0}^{\infty} f(x) d x \) exists.
Remark. The problem was first treated by G. H. Hardy, in Messenger of Mathematics, vol. 31, 1902, p. 177. He showed that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{\mu}}{1+x^{\nu} \sin ^{2} x} d x
\]
converges if and only if \( \nu>2 \mu+2 \).

descriptive_long.question:
11. Sketch the curve
\[
variabley=\frac{variablex}{1+variablex^{6} \sin ^{2} variablex}
\]
and show that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{variablex d variablex}{1+variablex^{6} \sin ^{2} variablex}
\]
exists.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
functionf(variablex)=\frac{variablex}{1+variablex^{6} \sin ^{2} variablex}
\]

Then \( functionf \) is an odd function, so its graph is symmetric with respect to the origin; hence we need consider only non-negative values of \( variablex \).

Clearly
\[
variablex \geq \frac{variablex}{1+variablex^{6} \sin ^{2} variablex} \geq \frac{variablex}{1+variablex^{6}} .
\]

At points \( variablex=indexint \pi, indexint \) an integer, the curve is tangent to the line \( variabley=variablex \). At points \( variablex=\left(indexint+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \), it is tangent to the graph of \( variabley=variablex /\left(1+variablex^{6}\right) \).

So the graph of \( functionf(variablex) \) oscillates between an upper curve \( variabley=variablex \) and a lower curve \( variabley=variablex /\left(1+variablex^{6}\right) \).

For large values of \( variablex \), except those very near an integral multiple of \( \pi \), \( functionf \) is very small, i.e., near the lower curve. Indeed, the graph is characterized by tall, narrow spikes at integral multiples of \( \pi \). (The spikes are much narrower than the drawing suggests. For example, the function \( functionf \) drops to less than .02 within .1 of \( 2 \pi \).)
Since \( functionf \) is non-negative on the whole domain of integration, the question of the convergence of
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} functionf(variablex) d variablex
\]
is equivalent to whether or not
\[
\int_{0}^{variablet} functionf(variablex) d variablex
\]
is bounded, and in turn to whether or not
\[
\sum_{indexint=1}^{\infty} \int_{(indexint-1 / 2) \pi}^{(indexint+1 / 2) \pi} functionf(variablex) d variablex
\]
is convergent.
Note that \( |\sin variablet| \geq(2 / \pi)|variablet| \) for \( |variablet| \leq \pi / 2 \). Set \( largescaling=2 \pi^{2}\left(indexint-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3} \). Then, for any positive integer \( indexint \), and for
\[
\left(indexint-\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \leq variablex \leq\left(indexint+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+variablex^{6} \sin ^{2} variablex & =1+variablex^{6} \sin ^{2}(variablex-indexint \pi) \\
& \geq 1+\left(indexint-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{6} \pi^{6} \frac{4}{\pi^{2}}(variablex-indexint \pi)^{2} \\
& =1+largescaling^{2}(variablex-indexint \pi)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

So we get
\[
functionf(variablex) \leq \frac{\left(indexint+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi}{1+largescaling^{2}(variablex-indexint \pi)^{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{r}
\int_{(indexint-1 / 2) \pi}^{(indexint+1 / 2) \pi} functionf(variablex) d variablex \leq\left(indexint+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\pi 2}^{\pi 2} \frac{d variableu}{1+largescaling{ }^{2} variableu^{2}} \\
\leq\left(indexint+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d variableu}{1+largescaling{ }^{2} variableu^{2}} \\
=\frac{\left(indexint+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi^{2}}{largescaling} \leq \frac{constanta}{indexint^{2}}
\end{array}
\]
for some number \( constanta \) independent of \( indexint \).
Since \( \sum_{indexint=1}^{\infty} 1 / indexint^{2} \) is convergent, we conclude that
\[
\sum_{indexint=1}^{\infty} \int_{(indexint-1 / 2) \pi}^{(indexint+1 / 2) \pi} functionf(variablex) d variablex
\]
is convergent.
Therefore \( \int_{0}^{\infty} functionf(variablex) d variablex \) exists.
Remark. The problem was first treated by G. H. Hardy, in Messenger of Mathematics, vol. 31, 1902, p. 177. He showed that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{variablex^{exponentmu}}{1+variablex^{exponentnu} \sin ^{2} variablex} d variablex
\]
converges if and only if \( exponentnu>2 exponentmu+2 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
11. Sketch the curve
\[
campfires=\frac{marigolds}{1+marigolds^{6} \sin ^{2} marigolds}
\]
and show that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{marigolds\,d marigolds}{1+marigolds^{6} \sin ^{2} marigolds}
\]
exists.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
sugarloaf(marigolds)=\frac{marigolds}{1+marigolds^{6} \sin ^{2} marigolds}
\]
Then \( sugarloaf \) is an odd function, so its graph is symmetric with respect to the origin; hence we need consider only non-negative values of \( marigolds \).

Clearly
\[
marigolds \geq \frac{marigolds}{1+marigolds^{6} \sin ^{2} marigolds} \geq \frac{marigolds}{1+marigolds^{6}} .
\]

At points \( marigolds=driftwood \pi, driftwood \) an integer, the curve is tangent to the line \( campfires=marigolds \). At points \( marigolds=\left(driftwood+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \), it is tangent to the graph of \( campfires=marigolds /\left(1+marigolds^{6}\right) \).

So the graph of \( sugarloaf(marigolds) \) oscillates between an upper curve \( campfires=marigolds \) and a lower curve \( campfires=marigolds /\left(1+marigolds^{0}\right) \).

For large values of \( marigolds \), except those very near an integral multiple of \( \pi \), \( sugarloaf \) is very small, i.e., near the lower curve. Indeed, the graph is characterized by tall, narrow spikes at integral multiples of \( \pi \). (The spikes are much narrower than the drawing suggests. For example, the function \( sugarloaf \) drops to less than .02 within .1 of \( 2 \pi \).)

Since \( sugarloaf \) is non-negative on the whole domain of integration, the question of the convergence of
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} sugarloaf(marigolds) \, d marigolds
\]
is equivalent to whether or not
\[
\int_{0}^{ploughman} sugarloaf(marigolds) \, d marigolds
\]
is bounded, and in turn to whether or not
\[
\sum_{driftwood=1}^{\infty} \int_{(driftwood-1 / 2) \pi}^{(driftwood+1 / 2) \pi} sugarloaf(marigolds) \, d marigolds
\]
is convergent.

Note that \( |\sin ploughman| \geq(2 / \pi)|ploughman| \) for \( |ploughman| \leq \pi / 2 \). Set \( lighthouse_{driftwood}=2 \pi^{2}\left(driftwood-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3} \). Then, for any positive integer \( driftwood \), and for
\[
\left(driftwood-\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \leq marigolds \leq\left(driftwood+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+marigolds^{6} \sin ^{2} marigolds & =1+marigolds^{6} \sin ^{2}(marigolds-driftwood \pi) \\
& \geq 1+\left(driftwood-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{6} \pi^{6} \frac{4}{\pi^{2}}(marigolds-driftwood \pi)^{2} \\
& =1+lighthouse_{driftwood}^{2}(marigolds-driftwood \pi)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

So we get
\[
sugarloaf(marigolds) \leq \frac{\left(driftwood+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi}{1+lighthouse_{driftwood}^{2}(marigolds-driftwood \pi)^{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{r}
\int_{(driftwood-1 / 2) \pi}^{(driftwood+1 / 2) \pi} sugarloaf(marigolds) \, d marigolds \leq\left(driftwood+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\pi 2}^{\pi 2} \frac{d rainstorm}{1+lighthouse_{driftwood}{ }^{2} rainstorm^{2}} \\
\leq\left(driftwood+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d rainstorm}{1+lighthouse_{driftwood}{ }^{2} rainstorm^{2}} \\
=\frac{\left(driftwood+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi^{2}}{lighthouse_{driftwood}} \leq \frac{waterfall}{driftwood^{2}}
\end{array}
\]
for some number \( waterfall \) independent of \( driftwood \).

Since \( \sum_{driftwood=1}^{\infty} 1 / driftwood^{2} \) is convergent, we conclude that
\[
\sum_{driftwood=1}^{\infty} \int_{(driftwood-1 / 2) \pi}^{(driftwood+1 / 2) \pi} sugarloaf(marigolds) \, d marigolds
\]
is convergent.

Therefore \( \int_{0}^{\infty} sugarloaf(marigolds) \, d marigolds \) exists.

Remark. The problem was first treated by G. H. Hardy, in Messenger of Mathematics, vol. 31, 1902, p. 177. He showed that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{marigolds^{barleycorn}}{1+marigolds^{ivorytower} \sin ^{2} marigolds} \, d marigolds
\]
converges if and only if \( ivorytower>2\,barleycorn+2 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
11. Sketch the curve
\[
independentvalue=\frac{dependentvalue}{1+dependentvalue^{6} \sin ^{2} dependentvalue}
\]
and show that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dependentvalue d dependentvalue}{1+dependentvalue^{6} \sin ^{2} dependentvalue}
\]
exists.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
constantvalue(dependentvalue)=\frac{dependentvalue}{1+dependentvalue^{6} \sin ^{2} dependentvalue}
\]

Then \( constantvalue \) is an odd function, so its graph is symmetric with respect to the origin; hence we need consider only non-negative values of \( dependentvalue \).

Clearly
\[
dependentvalue \geq \frac{dependentvalue}{1+dependentvalue^{6} \sin ^{2} dependentvalue} \geq \frac{dependentvalue}{1+dependentvalue^{6}} .
\]

At points \( dependentvalue=continuousparameter \pi, continuousparameter \) an integer, the curve is tangent to the line \( independentvalue=dependentvalue \). At points \( dependentvalue=\left(continuousparameter+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \), it is tangent to the graph of \( independentvalue=dependentvalue /\left(1+dependentvalue^{6}\right) \).

So the graph of \( constantvalue(dependentvalue) \) oscillates between an upper curve \( independentvalue=dependentvalue \) and a lower curve \( independentvalue=dependentvalue /\left(1+dependentvalue^{0}\right) \).

For large values of \( dependentvalue \), except those very near an integral multiple of \( \pi \), \( constantvalue \) is very small, i.e., near the lower curve. Indeed, the graph is characterized by tall, narrow spikes at integral multiples of \( \pi \). (The spikes are much narrower than the drawing suggests. For example, the function \( constantvalue \) drops to less than .02 within .1 of \( 2 \pi \).)
Since \( constantvalue \) is non-negative on the whole domain of integration, the question of the convergence of
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} constantvalue(dependentvalue) d dependentvalue
\]
is equivalent to whether or not
\[
\int_{0}^{anglelessquantity} constantvalue(dependentvalue) d dependentvalue
\]
is bounded, and in turn to whether or not
\[
\sum_{continuousparameter=1}^{\infty} \int_{(continuousparameter-1 / 2) \pi}^{(continuousparameter+1 / 2) \pi} constantvalue(dependentvalue) d dependentvalue
\]
is convergent.
Note that \( |\sin anglelessquantity| \geq(2 / \pi)|anglelessquantity| \) for \( |anglelessquantity| \leq \pi / 2 \). Set \( flexiblescalar_{continuousparameter}=2 \pi^{2}\left(continuousparameter-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3} \). Then, for any positive integer \( continuousparameter \), and for
\[
\left(continuousparameter-\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \leq dependentvalue \leq\left(continuousparameter+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+dependentvalue^{6} \sin ^{2} dependentvalue & =1+dependentvalue^{6} \sin ^{2}(dependentvalue-continuousparameter \pi) \\
& \geq 1+\left(continuousparameter-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{6} \pi^{6} \frac{4}{\pi^{2}}(dependentvalue-continuousparameter \pi)^{2} \\
& =1+flexiblescalar_{continuousparameter}^{2}(dependentvalue-continuousparameter \pi)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

So we get
\[
constantvalue(dependentvalue) \leq \frac{\left(continuousparameter+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi}{1+flexiblescalar_{continuousparameter}^{2}(dependentvalue-continuousparameter \pi)^{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{r}
\int_{(continuousparameter-1 / 2) \pi}^{(continuousparameter+1 / 2) \pi} constantvalue(dependentvalue) d dependentvalue \leq\left(continuousparameter+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\pi 2}^{\pi 2} \frac{d discreteindex}{1+flexiblescalar_{continuousparameter}^{2} discreteindex^{2}} \\
\leq\left(continuousparameter+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d discreteindex}{1+flexiblescalar_{continuousparameter}^{2} discreteindex^{2}} \\
=\frac{\left(continuousparameter+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi^{2}}{flexiblescalar_{continuousparameter}} \leq \frac{varyinglimit}{continuousparameter^{2}}
\end{array}
\]
for some number \( varyinglimit \) independent of \( continuousparameter \).
Since \( \sum_{continuousparameter=1}^{\infty} 1 / continuousparameter^{2} \) is convergent, we conclude that
\[
\sum_{continuousparameter=1}^{\infty} \int_{(continuousparameter-1 / 2) \pi}^{(continuousparameter+1 / 2) \pi} constantvalue(dependentvalue) d dependentvalue
\]
is convergent.
Therefore \( \int_{0}^{\infty} constantvalue(dependentvalue) d dependentvalue \) exists.
Remark. The problem was first treated by G. H. Hardy, in Messenger of Mathematics, vol. 31, 1902, p. 177. He showed that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dependentvalue^{radicalvalue}}{1+dependentvalue^{logarithmorder} \sin ^{2} dependentvalue} d dependentvalue
\]
converges if and only if \( logarithmorder>2 radicalvalue+2 \).

garbled_string.question:
11. Sketch the curve
\[
ljcbryun=\frac{qxzpmrtd}{1+qxzpmrtd^{6} \sin ^{2} qxzpmrtd}
\]
and show that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{qxzpmrtd \, d qxzpmrtd}{1+qxzpmrtd^{6} \sin ^{2} qxzpmrtd}
\]
exists.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let
\[
spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd)=\frac{qxzpmrtd}{1+qxzpmrtd^{6} \sin ^{2} qxzpmrtd}
\]

Then \( spqmdnva \) is an odd function, so its graph is symmetric with respect to the origin; hence we need consider only non-negative values of \( qxzpmrtd \).

Clearly
\[
qxzpmrtd \geq \frac{qxzpmrtd}{1+qxzpmrtd^{6} \sin ^{2} qxzpmrtd} \geq \frac{qxzpmrtd}{1+qxzpmrtd^{6}} .
\]

At points \( qxzpmrtd=hsgdplma \pi, hsgdplma \) an integer, the curve is tangent to the line \( ljcbryun=qxzpmrtd \). At points \( qxzpmrtd=\left(hsgdplma+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \), it is tangent to the graph of \( ljcbryun=qxzpmrtd /\left(1+qxzpmrtd^{6}\right) \).

So the graph of \( spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) \) oscillates between an upper curve \( ljcbryun=qxzpmrtd \) and a lower curve \( ljcbryun=qxzpmrtd /\left(1+qxzpmrtd^{0}\right) \).

For large values of \( qxzpmrtd \), except those very near an integral multiple of \( \pi \), \( spqmdnva \) is very small, i.e., near the lower curve. Indeed, the graph is characterized by tall, narrow spikes at integral multiples of \( \pi \). (The spikes are much narrower than the drawing suggests. For example, the function \( spqmdnva \) drops to less than .02 within .1 of \( 2 \pi \).)

Since \( spqmdnva \) is non-negative on the whole domain of integration, the question of the convergence of
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) d qxzpmrtd
\]
is equivalent to whether or not
\[
\int_{0}^{krzyploq} spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) d qxzpmrtd
\]
is bounded, and in turn to whether or not
\[
\sum_{hsgdplma=1}^{\infty} \int_{(hsgdplma-1 / 2) \pi}^{(hsgdplma+1 / 2) \pi} spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) d qxzpmrtd
\]
is convergent.

Note that \( |\sin krzyploq| \geq(2 / \pi)|krzyploq| \) for \( |krzyploq| \leq \pi / 2 \). Set \( nvtqzxea=2 \pi^{2}\left(hsgdplma-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3} \). Then, for any positive integer \( hsgdplma \), and for
\[
\left(hsgdplma-\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \leq qxzpmrtd \leq\left(hsgdplma+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1+qxzpmrtd^{6} \sin ^{2} qxzpmrtd & =1+qxzpmrtd^{6} \sin ^{2}(qxzpmrtd-hsgdplma \pi) \\
& \geq 1+\left(hsgdplma-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{6} \pi^{6} \frac{4}{\pi^{2}}(qxzpmrtd-hsgdplma \pi)^{2} \\
& =1+nvtqzxea^{2}(qxzpmrtd-hsgdplma \pi)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

So we get
\[
spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) \leq \frac{\left(hsgdplma+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi}{1+nvtqzxea^{2}(qxzpmrtd-hsgdplma \pi)^{2}}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{r}
\int_{(hsgdplma-1 / 2) \pi}^{(hsgdplma+1 / 2) \pi} spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) d qxzpmrtd \leq\left(hsgdplma+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\pi 2}^{\pi 2} \frac{d vdawerui}{1+nvtqzxea^{2} vdawerui^{2}} \\
\leq\left(hsgdplma+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d vdawerui}{1+nvtqzxea^{2} vdawerui^{2}} \\
=\frac{\left(hsgdplma+\frac{1}{2}\right) \pi^{2}}{nvtqzxea} \leq \frac{brfiklqw}{hsgdplma^{2}}
\end{array}
\]
for some number \( brfiklqw \) independent of \( hsgdplma \).

Since \( \sum_{hsgdplma=1}^{\infty} 1 / hsgdplma^{2} \) is convergent, we conclude that
\[
\sum_{hsgdplma=1}^{\infty} \int_{(hsgdplma-1 / 2) \pi}^{(hsgdplma+1 / 2) \pi} spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) d qxzpmrtd
\]
is convergent.
Therefore \( \int_{0}^{\infty} spqmdnva(qxzpmrtd) d qxzpmrtd \) exists.

Remark. The problem was first treated by G. H. Hardy, in Messenger of Mathematics, vol. 31, 1902, p. 177. He showed that
\[
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{qxzpmrtd^{pdnzqray}}{1+qxzpmrtd^{ctargwle} \sin ^{2} qxzpmrtd} d qxzpmrtd
\]
converges if and only if \( ctargwle>2 pdnzqray+2 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix the two real parameters  

  m \geq  4  and  \alpha  > 3.  

For x \in  \mathbb{R} set  
  F_{m,\alpha }(x)=\dfrac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2m}\sin^{2}x}+\dfrac{\sin x}{1+x^{\alpha }}.  

(a)  Give a qualitative sketch of y = F_{m,\alpha }(x), pointing out its parity, its behaviour near the origin, and the form of the alternating spikes that occur close to the points x = n\pi , n \in  \mathbb{Z}.  

(b)  Show that the improper integral  

  I_{m,\alpha }=\int _{0}^{\infty }|F_{m,\alpha }(x)|\,dx  

exists for every m \geq  4 and \alpha  > 3.  

(c)  Prove that, for fixed m, the map \alpha \mapsto I_{m,\alpha } is continuous on (3,\infty ).
kernel_variant.solution:
(a)  Preliminaries.  Because sin is odd and the quotients involve only even powers of x, one checks immediately that F_{m,\alpha }(-x)=-F_{m,\alpha }(x); hence the graph is centrally symmetric and it suffices to discuss x \geq  0.  Near the origin we have sin x\approx x, so  

 F_{m,\alpha }(x)=x^{2}+O(x^{4})+O(x),  

and therefore the curve leaves the origin tangentially to y = x^2.  
At x=n\pi  the sine term vanishes; the first summand equals (n\pi )^2, the second vanishes, so tall positive or negative spikes of height ~(n\pi )^2 appear.  Writing x=n\pi +t with |t|\ll 1 and using sin t\approx t,   

 F_{m,\alpha }(x)\approx \dfrac{(n\pi )^2}{1+(n\pi )^{2m}t^2}+O\!\bigl((n\pi )^{-(\alpha -1)}\bigr),  

we see that the spikes have width comparable to (n\pi )^{-m}.  Between spikes, |sin x|\geq sin(\pi /4)=1/\sqrt{2}, so the first summand is O(x^{2-2m}) and the second is O(x^{-\alpha }).  Since 2-2m\leq -6 and -\alpha \leq -4, the mid-level of the graph decays at least like x^{-4}.  

(b)  Absolute convergence.  We split the half-line into three disjoint sets:

E_0=[0,1],  A_n=[n\pi -\pi /4,n\pi +\pi /4] (n\geq 1),  and  M=\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\setminus(E_0\cup \bigcup A_n).

Step 1: the origin.  On E_0 we have |F_{m,\alpha }(x)|\leq x^2+x, so \int _{E_0}|F_{m,\alpha }|\leq 2/3.  

Step 2: the spikes.  For x\in A_n write x=n\pi +t, |t|\leq \pi /4.  By |sin t|\geq (2/\pi )|t| we get  

 1+x^{2m}\sin^2x \geq  1+c_n^2t^2, c_n=\dfrac{2(n\pi -\pi /4)^{m}}{\pi }.  

Moreover x^2\leq (n\pi +\pi /4)^2.  Hence  

 |F_{m,\alpha }(x)|\leq \dfrac{(n\pi +\pi /4)^2}{1+c_n^2t^2}+C(n\pi )^{-\alpha }, C constant.  

Integrating first term over t gives  

 \int _{A_n}\leq \pi ^2(n\pi +\pi /4)^2/(2c_n)=O(n^{2-m}),  

while the second term contributes O(n^{1-\alpha }).  Because m \geq  4 and \alpha  > 3, the series \Sigma _n O(n^{2-m})+O(n^{1-\alpha }) converges; hence \Sigma _n\int _{A_n}|F_{m,\alpha }| converges.  

Step 3: the mid-region.  On M we have |sin x|\geq 1/\sqrt{2}, so  

 |F_{m,\alpha }(x)|\leq 2x^{2-2m}+x^{-\alpha }.  

Both integrands are dominated by Cx^{-4}, and \int _{1}^{\infty }x^{-4}dx=1/3.  

Combining the three steps we conclude I_{m,\alpha }<\infty .  

(c)  Continuity in \alpha .  Fix m.  From the estimate in Step 3 we already know |F_{m,\alpha }(x)|\leq Cx^{-4} for x\geq 1, C independent of \alpha .  On [0,1] the integrand is bounded by 2.  Hence we may apply the dominated convergence theorem: if \alpha _k\to \alpha , then F_{m,\alpha _k}(x)\to F_{m,\alpha }(x) pointwise and the uniform bound above serves as a global integrable majorant.  It follows that I_{m,\alpha _k}\to I_{m,\alpha }, establishing continuity. \blacksquare
